posts.text: Obviously there are a bunch of complications (storage) and intermediate steps (refining, distribution) but in some sense if the global flow of crude oil falls 15% then prices need to rise high enough to cut consumption by 15%, which seems like it would be a very high price.
posts.quote: Obviously there are a bunch of complications (storage) and intermediate steps (refining, distribution) but in some sense if the global flow of crude oil falls 15% then prices need to rise high enough to cut consumption by 15%, which seems like it would be a very high price.

posts.text: I hesitate to try to out-guess financial markets, but $100/barrel oil seems low relative to the scale of the supply disruption and what we know about short-term elasticity of demand.
posts.quote: I hesitate to try to out-guess financial markets, but $100/barrel oil seems low relative to the scale of the supply disruption and what we know about short-term elasticity of demand.

posts.text:
Should we *require* new mixed use projects to look like the most celebrated boulevards of 19th century Europe? 

Probably not. But why are we prohibiting it?
posts.quote:
Should we *require* new mixed use projects to look like the most celebrated boulevards of 19th century Europe? 

Probably not. But why are we prohibiting it?

posts.text:
There’s nothing about the construction technology of a wood frame on top of a concrete podium that requires projects to look like we see on the right. 

The issue is “anti-massing” rules which presume that Paris is full of ugly streets.
posts.quote:
There’s nothing about the construction technology of a wood frame on top of a concrete podium that requires projects to look like we see on the right. 

The issue is “anti-massing” rules which presume that Paris is full of ugly streets.

posts.text:
I think we should say “five over ones are fine, and we should try to make them more attractive by encouraging builders to give them more traditional-looking façades rather than the current design standards that strike most people as ugly.”


Mark Gibson (@Delmarkva)

... as @GaryWinslett points out: "... Five-Over-Ones are glorious! ... They represent walkable neighborhoods where street-level retail thrives because there are enough residents to support local businesses." therebuild.pub/p/in-defe
